title: Personal Thoughts on Episode 24
wikitext:
[]: Personal Thoughts on Episode 24             Quote Post by ran1 » Mon Apr 12, 2010 7:16 pm I think it was Episode 24, specifically the latter half of the episode, in which Kaworou's identity is revealed, and then attacks NERV, that Evangelion transcended in my mind from the status of "good anime" to "work of art worthy of study". In that latter half, I found everything I was looking for in cinema, the flawless combination of soundtrack, image, character, narrative -- they all culminated into something that for me, was emotionally moving, something that does not occur often to me in the medium of film/animation. I'm often reminded of the scene when I watch films like Kurosawa's Kagemusha , Kubrick's A Clockwork Orange or Forman's Amadeus . Its basically what Kurosawa would've called "pure cinema", this combination of all the elements in film in an almost perfect manner. === At some point, I'd like to a full deconstruction of that scene in a similar breakdown to the Episode 22 montages-- but before I embark, I'd like to know: did Episode 24 leave a lasting impression on you, specifically in its latter half? If not, could you pinpoint another point in the series that did? I'm curious.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Mon Apr 12, 2010 7:29 pm I thought the music and visuals were spot-on, but the Shinji/Kaworu relationship was far too rushed for me to really care about his death (felt cheesy, tbh) and some parts of the plot still don't make any damned sense.

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Mon Apr 12, 2010 7:41 pm NemZ wrote: I thought the music and visuals were spot-on, but the Shinji/Kaworu relationship was far too rushed for me to really care about his death (felt cheesy, tbh)

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Mon Apr 12, 2010 7:46 pm Oh, just the infamous "wait, this isn't my body!" problem that we've spent several threads discussing without making much headway.

[]:             Quote Post by planet news » Mon Apr 12, 2010 7:46 pm I know this is not a discussion on Kaworu/Shinji, but everything that we know about Shinji made that relationship totally believable. I still think the minute-long still shot is one of the most genius things I've ever seen. Don't know why, but I was just amazed by it the first time I saw it and still am. I also liked when the Evas were crashing through the various level before Terminal Dogma, how they were passing through these strange "decorative" environments. Only music/visual sequence I might like better is the EoE Asuka "berserk".

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Mon Apr 12, 2010 7:52 pm ^ See, I really didn't take that to really be about Kaworu so much as it was proving that Shinji learned his lesson from ep18 when he almost doomed the world because of his moral idealism.

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Mon Apr 12, 2010 8:26 pm Well, I can't really say that Episode #24 has all that much of an emotional impact on me, but looking back on the episode I can see somewhat where you're coming from. As rushed as the first half and the introduction of Kaworu feel, I still think the "descent" sequence is actually incredibly well choreographed and paced. It's a riveting view every single time I watch it . All NGE is there: Action, angst, Evas,, enigmas, technobabble, technology, tension, sideplots, subplots, subtexts, suspense, philosophy, plot-twists, Ode to Joy, cryptic allusions to kabballistic backstories, and of course Gendo. It's probably one of the best 7-8 minutes of the whole show. It's not my favourite sequence though. My personal best moment of Evangelion is in fact Ireul's hacking attempt in #13. The entire sequence consists entirely of operators sitting at their desk clacking at keyboards, yet its one of the most thrilling and dramatic sequences in the series. It's suspenseful, energetic, original, well paced, has a great soundtrack, and is very "readable" if that's the right word. In fact, I think it's probably one of the best "hacking sequences" ever made. As you can tell, I really like episode #13, despite the fact that its 99 parts nonsensical technobabble. How many other shows make a computer hacking attempt this much fun!? I suppose everyone has their favourite sequence in the show. I think there's a great moment in it for just about anybody.

[]:             Quote Post by Seele00TextOnly » Tue Apr 13, 2010 5:27 am ...

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Tue Apr 13, 2010 12:30 pm The only flaw of Episode 24 is that it was so short. It is, quite possibly, the most important episode in the entire series, completely encapsulating and containing the entirety of the Tragedy of Shinji in what is basically a short and beautiful 17 minutes.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Tue Apr 13, 2010 3:07 pm AuraTwilight wrote: The only flaw of Episode 24 is that it was so short.

[]:             Quote Post by planet news » Tue Apr 13, 2010 3:35 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: I think it could have been split into 3 episodes (24, 25, 26), and no episodes of the mental reactions to Instrumentality.

[]:             Quote Post by Heromaster111 » Tue Apr 13, 2010 3:39 pm Kaworu was Eva's Smith-kun.

[]:             Quote Post by DIDDY » Tue Apr 13, 2010 4:09 pm ran1 wrote: I always felt that the depth of their relationship was one based on off-screen implications. I see where you are coming from, though. I guess its dependent on how much one can suspend certain amounts of disbelief.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Tue Apr 13, 2010 4:33 pm I do feel otherwise. Yes it might have meant something to Shinji, but killing the cryptic weirdo who only showed up this very episode doesn't resonate with me as a viewer even in the slightest. During the long pause I was basically growing progressively more frustrated that Shinji was still hesitating.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Tue Apr 13, 2010 5:11 pm I remember my initial experience of episode #24 was ruined because my brother told me that "the last Angel tries to have sex with Shinji". It took quite a while for me to overcome my Kaworuphobia. The episode itself? The whole ending sequence is pretty epic. There's a lot about it that leaves a lasting impression. Though, ever since somebody noted that the Evas are basically "holding onto each other", I've felt like the actual fighting part feels half-assed. Anno: "Blast the Beethoven loud enough and have the creepy kid say enough stuff that makes no sense, and most of the audience won't notice that barely anything is actually happening... even in the upgraded version of the episode!" Random Minion: "Your directorial trickery never fails, Anno-sempai!" I also hold to the opinion that much of the writing in the episode is needlessly opaque and hopelessly incoherent, even by NGE's standards. Oh, we can fanwank to our hearts' content, and Trust In Anno & Friends to have done us the courtesy of providing something that might ultimately be coherent... But, yeah ... I don't think I can take this show so seriously, or dare consider it so clean of flaws (HAH! HAH HAH!!!) , that I won't seriously consider the possibility that the writing has weak spots that readily break away to reveal plot holes Sahaquiel could float through. [/Jimbo Summon Spell] The most the whole Kaworu/Shinji relationship ever impacted me was when I suffered a phase of hypomania and had temporarily convinced myself that Kaworu was female. No, this is not merely an urban legend; it really did happen. As such, I briefly believed that Kaworu's bond to Shinji was beautiful and maternal and also extended to levels that I'm sure Synapsid would appreciate. Since I did eventually revert to my normal sanity levels, the "Kaworu is female" thing got put on a "that's awesome and fucked up; I think I should write fanfic about it" shelf, and, as my eyes cleared from the manic haze, I realized what I may have already known from before: that there was nothing especially beautiful about the Shinji/Kaworu relationship. Shinji was weak and selfish and has the gall to claim he was betrayed by some weird stranger he knew nothing about, nor even cared to ask. Kaworu decided to fuck over the Angels (effectively "his" children) because this weak, selfish kid somehow managed to persuade him that this race of humans bent on self-destruction was worth terminating a maternal mission billions of years old, and accepting death as being for some reason just as good as life (some Seed you've turned out to be...). Yeah...

[]:             Quote Post by DIDDY » Tue Apr 13, 2010 11:15 pm NemZ wrote: I do feel otherwise. Yes it might have meant something to Shinji, but killing the cryptic weirdo who only showed up this very episode doesn't resonate with me as a viewer even in the slightest. During the long pause I was basically growing progressively more frustrated that Shinji was still hesitating.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Tue Apr 13, 2010 11:28 pm DIDDY wrote: I'm surprised, and perhaps confused. You wanted to see more Kaworu? Let me sort this out: how would you feel about no Kaworu at all?

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Tue Apr 13, 2010 11:32 pm DIDDY wrote: I thought it was kind of sweet.

[]:             Quote Post by Legendary » Tue Apr 13, 2010 11:37 pm You can draw a lot out of Kaworu's character. The problem is that there's LOTS of possible interpretations because there's little in the realm of concrete evidence. There's still a contingent of Eva fans who find Kaworu's last act not to be a sacrifice for Shinji but him fucking with Shinji. I myself find that Kaworu could probably have been better implemented in a more manga-esque fashion, being introduced slightly earlier on and interacting on a more meaningful level than the 17 minutes he got. 24 was nice though. I think that you could fly a fleet of Sahaquiel through the plot hole 24' made, but w/e.

[]:             Quote Post by Nukie » Wed Apr 14, 2010 12:11 am NemZ wrote: I do feel otherwise. Yes it might have meant something to Shinji, but killing the cryptic weirdo who only showed up this very episode doesn't resonate with me as a viewer even in the slightest. During the long pause I was basically growing progressively more frustrated that Shinji was still hesitating.
